column2: AI Company Security Incident Ledger (as of 2026-07-10)

column9: scope_summary
column13: summary

column9: A Redis client library bug caused a small percentage of users to see another user's payment-related information and chat history titles. Approximately 1.2% of ChatGPT Plus subscribers who were active during a specific window were affected.
column13: OpenAI disclosed that a bug in the open-source Redis client library redis-py caused ChatGPT to show some users' chat titles and, for a smaller subset, payment-related information (first and last name, email, payment address, last four digits of a credit card, and expiration date) belonging to other active users during a nine-hour window on March 20, 2023.
column14: OpenAI took ChatGPT offline, patched the Redis library, and published a detailed incident report. They notified affected users.

column9: Lasso Security researchers found 1,681 valid HuggingFace API tokens exposed in public GitHub repositories and HuggingFace Spaces. Tokens belonged to organizations including Meta, Google, Microsoft, and VMware. Some were write-access tokens that could have allowed model poisoning.
column13: Lasso Security published research documenting 1,681 valid HuggingFace API tokens found in public GitHub repositories and HuggingFace Spaces. The exposed tokens included write-access credentials belonging to employees at Meta, Google, Microsoft, and VMware, among others. Write-access tokens could have allowed an attacker to modify models hosted on the HuggingFace Hub.
column14: HuggingFace acknowledged the findings and worked with Lasso Security to revoke exposed tokens.

column9: Oligo Security documented active exploitation of CVE-2023-48022 across thousands of publicly exposed Ray servers used for AI workloads. Attackers accessed sensitive data including cloud credentials, models, and datasets, and hijacked compute for cryptocurrency mining; exploitation had been under way for roughly seven months before disclosure.
column13: Oligo Security published research showing that CVE-2023-48022, a disputed vulnerability in the Ray AI framework (Anyscale considered the lack of authentication to be by design rather than a vulnerability), was being actively exploited across thousands of publicly exposed Ray clusters. Attackers used the unauthenticated access to steal credentials, cloud tokens, and production data, and to deploy cryptocurrency miners.
column14: Anyscale maintained that Ray is designed to run in trusted environments and that the lack of built-in authentication is documented behavior, not a vulnerability. They published guidance on securing Ray deployments.

column9: Wiz researchers demonstrated that a malicious model running on Replicate's platform could escape its container and access other customers' AI models, prompts, and training data through cross-tenant isolation flaws.
column13: Wiz Research disclosed a cross-tenant vulnerability in Replicate's AI-as-a-service platform. By running a specially crafted model, a researcher could escape the container sandbox and access other tenants' models, prompts, and training data. Replicate patched the vulnerability within days of responsible disclosure.
column14: Replicate acknowledged the vulnerability, patched it within days, and published a security update.

column9: HuggingFace detected unauthorized access to its Spaces platform, specifically related to Spaces secrets. A subset of secrets may have been accessed without authorization. HuggingFace revoked affected tokens, engaged forensic specialists, and reported to law enforcement and data protection authorities.
column13: HuggingFace disclosed that it detected unauthorized access to its Spaces platform involving Spaces secrets (API tokens and keys stored by users); a subset may have been accessed. The company revoked affected HuggingFace tokens, engaged external cybersecurity forensic specialists, implemented a key management service for Spaces secrets, and reported the incident to law enforcement and data protection authorities.
column14: HuggingFace revoked compromised tokens, deprecated classic read/write tokens in favor of fine-grained access tokens, implemented KMS for Spaces secrets, and engaged external forensic specialists.

column3: Probllama path traversal and remote code execution
column9: CVE-2024-37032 allowed path traversal leading to arbitrary file write and remote code execution on Ollama instances. The vulnerability was particularly severe in Docker installations where the server runs as root and listens on 0.0.0.0 by default.
column13: Wiz Research disclosed CVE-2024-37032 (nicknamed Probllama), a path traversal vulnerability in Ollama that allowed arbitrary file write and remote code execution. The digest field in manifest files lacked proper validation, enabling an attacker to write files anywhere on the filesystem. In Docker installations, Ollama runs as root and listens on 0.0.0.0, making remote exploitation straightforward.
column14: Ollama committed a fix approximately four hours after receiving the initial report on May 5, 2024, and released patched version 0.1.34 on May 8, 2024.

column3: R1 leaked internal code and SaaS API keys
column9: Internal source code containing working SaaS API keys, leaked by an employee to a third-party group. Rabbit names the ElevenLabs key (access to bulk pseudo-anonymized text-to-speech data and global r1 voice settings) and the SendGrid key (customer-facing email sending), and states no customer data was exposed.
column13: A Rabbit employee leaked internal source code containing working SaaS API keys to a third-party group, following earlier hardcoded-key findings published by the Rabbitude research collective. Rabbit's investigation names the ElevenLabs and SendGrid keys and reports the only observed abuse of the keys was sending defamatory emails.
column14: Rabbit rotated and revoked the exposed secrets, migrated keys into AWS Secrets Manager, terminated the responsible employee, disabled ElevenLabs history logging, commissioned a third-party codebase audit, and added automated checks against committing secrets.

column9: Wiz researchers found multiple vulnerabilities in SAP AI Core that allowed cross-tenant access to other customers' AI models, training data, and cloud credentials (AWS, Azure, SAP HANA). The vulnerabilities chained together to achieve full tenant isolation bypass.
column13: Wiz Research disclosed a set of vulnerabilities in SAP AI Core, collectively named SAPwned, that could have let an attacker escape their tenant and access other customers' data, AI models, and cloud credentials. The demonstrated chain reached Kubernetes cluster access, AWS tokens, Azure credentials, and SAP HANA database credentials belonging to other tenants. No evidence of exploitation in the wild was reported at disclosure.
column14: SAP fixed all reported vulnerabilities. No evidence of exploitation in the wild was found at the time of disclosure.

column9: CVE-2024-38206 could let an attacker bypass SSRF protections in Microsoft Copilot Studio, access instance metadata and managed identity tokens, and reach internal resources including Cosmos DB instances.
column13: Tenable disclosed CVE-2024-38206, a server-side request forgery vulnerability in Microsoft Copilot Studio (on the Power Platform). An attacker could circumvent SSRF protections to access instance metadata, managed identity tokens, and internal resources including Cosmos DB. Microsoft rated the vulnerability as critical severity.
column14: Microsoft pushed fixes to all affected services and regions by late July 2024. No customer action was required. The CVE was published in August 2024.

column9: CVE-2024-0132 could let an attacker controlling a container image escape the container and gain full access to the underlying host system. All versions of NVIDIA Container Toolkit up to v1.16.1 and GPU Operator up to 24.6.1 were affected.
column13: Wiz Research disclosed CVE-2024-0132, a critical container escape vulnerability in NVIDIA Container Toolkit and GPU Operator. An attacker with control over a container image could escape the container and gain full access to the underlying host system, including the host filesystem. The vulnerability exploited the fact that Unix sockets remain writable even when mounted with read-only permissions.
column14: NVIDIA released patched versions (Container Toolkit v1.16.2 and GPU Operator v24.6.2) on September 26, 2024, the same day the vulnerability was publicly disclosed.

column9: A threat actor claimed to have breached Muah.AI, an AI companion chatbot platform, and the breach was verified by Have I Been Pwned. The exposed dataset contained 1.9 million user accounts.
column13: A threat actor claimed to have breached Muah.AI, an AI companion chatbot platform, in September 2024. Have I Been Pwned independently verified the breach data and added it on October 8, 2024, listing 1.9 million compromised accounts.

column9: Versions 8.3.41, 8.3.42, 8.3.45, and 8.3.46 of the Ultralytics Python package on PyPI were affected and removed. The initial compromise injected an XMRig cryptocurrency miner; the PyPI wheel differed from the corresponding GitHub source, indicating the publishing pipeline was the attack vector rather than the source repository.
column13: The Ultralytics YOLO computer vision library (a popular open-source AI package) had its PyPI publishing pipeline compromised, resulting in version 8.3.41 containing an XMRig cryptocurrency miner injected into utils/downloads.py. The malicious code was present only in the PyPI-published wheel, not in the GitHub source repository at the same tag, confirming a pipeline attack rather than a source code compromise.
column14: Ultralytics removed the compromised version from PyPI and published a fix via pull request #18111 on GitHub.

column3: Exposed ClickHouse database with user data
column9: Wiz Research discovered a publicly accessible, completely unauthenticated ClickHouse database belonging to DeepSeek containing over one million lines of log entries with plaintext chat histories, API secret keys, and backend operational details. Logs dated from January 6, 2025.
column13: Wiz Research discovered a publicly accessible ClickHouse database belonging to DeepSeek at oauth2callback.deepseek.com and dev.deepseek.com. The database required no authentication and contained over one million log entries including plaintext user chat histories, API secret keys, backend operational details, and internal API endpoint references. Wiz responsibly disclosed the issue and DeepSeek promptly secured the exposure.
column14: DeepSeek promptly secured the exposure after responsible disclosure by Wiz Research.

column3: Drift OAuth-token theft and downstream Salesforce data exfiltration
column9: A threat actor stole OAuth tokens for the Drift-Salesforce integration and used them between August 8 and August 18, 2025 to exfiltrate data from customers' Salesforce instances. FINRA characterized the incident as a supply-chain attack impacting more than 700 organizations. Salesloft's investigation found the attacker had accessed Salesloft's GitHub from March through June 2025 and reached Drift's AWS environment.
column13: Salesloft disclosed that a threat actor used OAuth credentials for its Drift AI chat-agent integration to exfiltrate data from customers' Salesforce instances between August 8 and August 18, 2025. Salesforce disabled the Drift and Salesloft integrations in response, and Salesloft's investigation with Mandiant traced the intrusion back to access to Salesloft's GitHub beginning in March 2025.
column14: Salesloft published trust-center updates, engaged Mandiant, rotated credentials, and worked with Salesforce, which disabled the Drift integrations while the incident was remediated.

column3: Mixpanel analytics-vendor breach affecting OpenAI API users
column9: An attacker gained unauthorized access to part of Mixpanel's systems and exported a dataset containing limited customer-identifiable analytics data from the frontend of OpenAI's API product: name, email address, coarse location, operating system and browser, referring websites, and OpenAI organization or user IDs.
column13: OpenAI disclosed that Mixpanel, a third-party analytics provider used on the frontend of its API product, detected an attacker who had gained unauthorized access to part of Mixpanel's systems on November 9, 2025 and exported a dataset that included limited identifiable information about OpenAI API users. The incident occurred in Mixpanel's systems, not OpenAI's.
column14: OpenAI removed Mixpanel from production, reviewed the affected dataset Mixpanel shared on November 25, notified affected users and organizations, and terminated its use of Mixpanel.

column9: Oligo Security documented a second wave of exploitation targeting Ray clusters, finding continued active compromise of AI infrastructure using the same CVE-2023-48022 attack vector with evolved techniques.
column13: Oligo Security published follow-up research documenting continued exploitation of Ray clusters using CVE-2023-48022, the same vulnerability from the original ShadowRay campaign. The second wave featured evolved attack techniques and demonstrated that many Ray deployments remained unprotected despite the original disclosure in March 2024.

column9: Mercor.io Corporation filed a data breach notice with the California Attorney General reporting incidents on March 24 and March 30, 2026. The filing is listed under breach report SB24-625431. The consumer notice PDF contains breach details.
column13: Mercor, an AI-powered hiring and talent marketplace platform, filed data breach notice SB24-625431 with the California Attorney General, disclosed June 25, 2026. The filing confirms breach dates of March 24 and March 30, 2026. Specific data elements and the number of affected Californians are detailed in the linked consumer notice PDF.

column3: Malicious Axios version in macOS app-signing workflow
column9: A GitHub Actions workflow used in OpenAI's macOS app-signing process downloaded and executed a malicious version of the Axios library (1.14.1) during a broader npm supply-chain attack. The workflow had access to code-signing and notarization material; OpenAI found no evidence that user data, systems, intellectual property, or shipped software were compromised or altered.
column13: OpenAI disclosed that on March 31, 2026 (UTC), a GitHub Actions workflow in its macOS app-signing pipeline executed malicious Axios version 1.14.1 as part of a broader software supply-chain attack. Out of caution, OpenAI treated its macOS code-signing certificate as compromised even though its analysis found no evidence of misuse.
column14: OpenAI rotated the macOS signing certificate, released newly signed builds, and required users to update the macOS app by May 8, 2026.

column9: A compromised version of a Mistral SDK package was published, containing unauthorized modifications. Mistral identified the compromise and published a security advisory with affected version details.
column13: Mistral disclosed that a version of one of its SDK packages was compromised and published with unauthorized modifications. The company identified the compromise, pulled the affected versions, and published a security advisory directing users to upgrade to a clean version.
column14: Mistral pulled the affected package versions and published a security advisory with upgrade instructions.
